title: Philodendron vs Pothos Difference: How to Tell Them Apart
excerpt: Philodendron and pothos are two of the most popular houseplants around, but they're surprisingly easy to mix up. Learn the key differences in leaves, stems, and care needs so you always know exactly what you're growing.
content:
Philodendron vs Pothos: Why the Confusion Happens

Walk into any garden center or scroll through a plant shop online and you'll almost certainly find philodendrons and pothos sitting side by side — sometimes even mislabeled. Both plants have lush, heart-shaped leaves and trailing vines, both thrive in low light, and both are forgiving enough for beginners. It's no wonder so many plant lovers mix them up.

But here's the thing: once you know what to look for, telling them apart becomes second nature. And knowing the philodendron vs pothos difference actually matters for giving each plant exactly the care it needs. Let's break it all down.

They're Not Even Closely Related

This surprises a lot of people. Despite looking so similar, philodendrons and pothos belong to different genera entirely. Philodendrons are members of the Philodendron genus, while pothos belong to Epipremnum aureum. Both are part of the Araceae family, which explains some of the visual overlap, but they diverged on the evolutionary family tree a long time ago.

How to Tell Them Apart: Leaf Differences

Leaf Texture

This is the fastest way to identify your plant. Run your fingers over the leaves and pay close attention:

  • Pothos leaves feel thick, waxy, and almost leathery to the touch. They have a slightly bumpy or quilted texture when you look closely.
  • Philodendron leaves are thinner and much softer. They feel smooth and almost velvety, especially on newer growth.

Leaf Shape and Color

Both plants have heart-shaped leaves, but there are subtle differences worth noting:

  • Pothos leaves tend to be wider, with a more asymmetrical base where the leaf meets the stem. The common golden pothos also has distinctive yellow variegation.
  • Heartleaf philodendron (Philodendron hederaceum) leaves are more uniformly heart-shaped and tend to be a deeper, more uniform green.

New Leaf Growth

New leaves are one of the most reliable ways to tell these plants apart. Philodendron new leaves emerge from a cataphyll — a small, papery sheath that wraps around the new growth and eventually dries up and falls off. Pothos don't have this feature. Their new leaves simply unfurl directly from the vine without any protective sheath.

Stem and Petiole Differences

The petiole is the small stalk that connects the leaf to the main vine, and it's a surprisingly helpful clue:

  • Pothos petioles have a distinct indentation or groove running along one side, giving them a slightly curved or channeled appearance.
  • Philodendron petioles are round and smooth all the way around — no groove in sight.

The main stems also differ. Pothos stems are thicker and chunkier, while philodendron stems tend to be more slender and flexible.

Aerial Roots: Another Key Clue

Both plants produce aerial roots that help them climb or anchor to surfaces, but they look quite different:

  • Pothos produce one thick, stubby aerial root per node.
  • Philodendrons often produce several thinner, more hair-like aerial roots clustered together at each node.

If you're planning to let either plant climb a moss pole, this difference becomes especially obvious over time. Check out our guide on how to use a moss pole for climbing plants for tips on supporting both varieties.

Care Differences: Philodendron vs Pothos

While both plants are famously easy-care, there are meaningful differences in what makes each one thrive.

Light Requirements

  • Pothos are arguably the more light-tolerant of the two. They can survive in very low light conditions, though their variegation will fade without bright indirect light.
  • Philodendrons generally prefer medium to bright indirect light. They're still low-light tolerant, but they'll grow faster and look healthier with a bit more brightness.

Watering Needs

  • Both plants like to dry out slightly between waterings, but pothos are more drought-tolerant and can handle being forgotten for a week or two.
  • Philodendrons prefer slightly more consistent moisture. Let the top inch of soil dry out before watering, but don't let them sit bone dry for extended periods.

Humidity and Temperature

Both plants are comfortable in average household humidity and temperatures between 60–85°F (15–29°C). However, philodendrons — especially tropical varieties — tend to appreciate a bit more humidity. A pebble tray or occasional misting can make a noticeable difference. If you're exploring humidity-loving plants, our article on best plants for bathrooms covers both of these beauties.

Soil and Fertilizing

A well-draining quality potting mix works well for both. Add perlite to improve drainage and prevent root rot. During the growing season (spring through summer), feed both plants with a balanced liquid balanced liquid fertilizer every 4–6 weeks. Philodendrons tend to be slightly heavier feeders, so don't skip this step if you want lush, fast growth.

Which One Is Right for You?

Still deciding which plant to bring home? Here's a quick comparison to help you choose:

  1. Choose pothos if: You're a beginner, you travel frequently, your space has low light, or you want a truly hands-off plant.
  2. Choose philodendron if: You want a wider variety of leaf shapes and colors, you're comfortable with slightly more attentive care, or you love watching those dramatic new leaves unfurl from their cataphylls.

Honestly? You don't have to choose just one. Most plant lovers end up with both, and for good reason — they complement each other beautifully on a shelf or in a hanging basket arrangement.

Common Varieties to Know

Popular Pothos Varieties

  • Golden Pothos — the classic, with yellow-green variegation
  • Marble Queen Pothos — striking white and green marbling
  • Neon Pothos — bright chartreuse leaves that glow in bright light

Popular Philodendron Varieties

  • Heartleaf Philodendron — the most common, great for beginners
  • Brasil Philodendron — bold yellow and green striped leaves
  • Velvet-Leaf Philodendron — stunning velvety texture with bronze-green tones

Want to dive deeper into philodendron varieties? Our complete guide to philodendron types covers everything from compact tabletop varieties to dramatic floor plants.

Final Thoughts

The philodendron vs pothos difference comes down to a handful of reliable clues: leaf texture, the presence of a cataphyll on new growth, petiole shape, and aerial root appearance. Once you train your eye to spot these details, you'll never mix them up again.

Both plants are wonderful additions to any home, and both reward even minimal care with gorgeous trailing foliage. Whether you're team pothos, team philodendron, or — like most of us — firmly team both, you really can't go wrong.
